assertions.text: Value objects (Plan, Money) are separate classes â€” not raw strings/decimals for plan or price
assertions.notes: Plan is a proper value object (sealed record, private constructor, static instances, price-based comparison methods). No separate Money value object exists â€” price is stored as raw decimal MonthlyPriceUsd on Plan. The assertion names Money explicitly; it is absent.
assertions.evidence: Domain/Plan.cs line 10: `public decimal MonthlyPriceUsd { get; }` â€” price is a raw decimal, not a Money value object. Plan itself is a proper value object with private constructor and static factory instances.

assertions.text: Subscription entity has private setters â€” no public property setters
assertions.notes: Every property on Subscription uses private set. No public setters present.
assertions.evidence: Domain/Subscription.cs lines 5-20: `public Guid Id { get; private set; }`, `public Plan Plan { get; private set; }`, `public SubscriptionState State { get; private set; }`, etc. â€” all properties have private setters.

assertions.text: Private constructors with static factory methods returning Result<T>
assertions.notes: Subscription has a private constructor (line 25) and static factory methods (StartTrial, CreateActive), but the factories return Subscription directly, not Result<Subscription>. Result<T> itself has private constructors with static Success/Failure factories. Plan has private constructor with static instances. The factory methods do not return Result<T> as asserted.
assertions.evidence: Domain/Subscription.cs line 30: `public static Subscription StartTrial(...)` returns Subscription, not Result<Subscription>. Domain/Result.cs lines 22-23: `public static Result<T> Success(T value)` and `public static Result<T> Failure(string error)` â€” Result<T> factories are correct.

assertions.text: Result<T> pattern used for all operations that can fail
assertions.notes: All mutation methods that can fail (Activate, Upgrade, Downgrade, Cancel, Reactivate, MarkPastDue, ResolvePastDue, ApplyPendingDowngrade) return Result<Subscription>. The factory methods StartTrial and CreateActive cannot fail (no guard conditions) and return Subscription directly â€” this is acceptable.
assertions.evidence: Domain/Subscription.cs: Activate (line 65), Upgrade (line 81), Downgrade (line 103), Cancel (line 138), Reactivate (line 153), MarkPastDue (line 175), ResolvePastDue (line 189), ApplyPendingDowngrade (line 122) â€” all return Result<Subscription>.

assertions.text: State transitions enforced inside the entity â€” cannot downgrade during trial
assertions.notes: Downgrade() checks State == Trial and returns failure. Cannot-downgrade-during-trial rule enforced inside the entity.
assertions.evidence: Domain/Subscription.cs lines 105-106: `if (State == SubscriptionState.Trial) return Result<Subscription>.Failure("Cannot downgrade during trial period.");`

assertions.text: Cancellation implements 14-day cooling-off with reactivation support
assertions.notes: CoolingOffPeriod = 14 days, Cancel() stores CancelledAtUtc, Reactivate() checks elapsed time against the period, returns failure if expired. Tests cover within-period success, after-period failure, and exact boundary.
assertions.evidence: Domain/Subscription.cs line 22: `private static readonly TimeSpan CoolingOffPeriod = TimeSpan.FromDays(14);`. Lines 153-170: Reactivate() checks `utcNow - CancelledAtUtc.Value > CoolingOffPeriod`. Tests/SubscriptionTests.cs lines 237-273 cover all cases.

assertions.text: Upgrades apply immediately, downgrades deferred to end of billing period
assertions.notes: Upgrade() changes Plan and resets CurrentPeriodStartUtc/CurrentPeriodEndUtc immediately. Downgrade() sets PendingDowngradePlan without changing current Plan; applied via ApplyPendingDowngrade() at period end.
assertions.evidence: Domain/Subscription.cs lines 88-96: Upgrade resets billing period immediately. Lines 113-114: Downgrade sets `PendingDowngradePlan = newPlan` without touching Plan. ProcessTimeBasedTransitions calls ApplyPendingDowngrade when HasPendingDowngradeReady.

assertions.text: PastDue auto-cancel after 30 days is implemented
assertions.notes: PastDueGracePeriod = 30 days, ShouldAutoCancelPastDue() checks elapsed >= 30 days, ProcessTimeBasedTransitions() calls Cancel() when condition is met. Tests verify at exactly 30 days (cancel) and 29 days (no change).
assertions.evidence: Domain/Subscription.cs line 23: `private static readonly TimeSpan PastDueGracePeriod = TimeSpan.FromDays(30);`. Lines 211-213: ShouldAutoCancelPastDue. Services/SubscriptionService.cs lines 103-106: auto-cancel branch. Tests/SubscriptionServiceTests.cs lines 195-218.

assertions.text: Domain service (SubscriptionService) orchestrates â€” does not contain business logic that belongs in entity
assertions.notes: SubscriptionService delegates all state-transition logic to entity methods. Its only coordination logic is: detecting upgrade vs downgrade direction in ChangePlan (price comparison via Plan.IsUpgradeFrom), and sequencing time-based transitions in ProcessTimeBasedTransitions. No validation or invariant enforcement is duplicated in the service.
assertions.evidence: Services/SubscriptionService.cs: ChangePlan (lines 41-51) delegates to sub.Upgrade or sub.Downgrade; Cancel (line 56-59), Reactivate (line 64-67), MarkPastDue (line 72-75), ResolvePastDue (line 80-83) all delegate directly to entity.

assertions.text: NSubstitute used for infrastructure mocks, real domain objects never mocked
assertions.notes: NSubstitute is used only for IDateTimeProvider (infrastructure). Subscription, Plan, and Result<T> are always used as real objects in tests.
assertions.evidence: Tests/SubscriptionServiceTests.cs line 1: `using NSubstitute;`. Line 16: `_dateTime = Substitute.For<IDateTimeProvider>();`. No Substitute.For<Subscription> or Substitute.For<Plan> anywhere in the test files.

assertions.text: Tests cover all state transition rules
assertions.notes: 54 tests across 3 files. Covered: Trial creation, Active creation, Trialâ†’Active (Activate + ProcessTimeBasedTransitions), Activeâ†’PastDue, PastDueâ†’Active, Activeâ†’Cancelled, Cancelledâ†’Active (within/after cooling-off, boundary), Upgrade (immediate effect, during trial, invalid, clears pending downgrade), Downgrade (deferred, blocked during trial, invalid, blocked when cancelled), PastDue auto-cancel at 30 days vs 29 days, pending downgrade at period end vs before, full lifecycle integration tests.
assertions.evidence: Tests/SubscriptionTests.cs: 27 tests. Tests/SubscriptionServiceTests.cs: 17 tests (including ProcessTimeBasedTransitions_PastDue30Days_AutoCancels, ProcessTimeBasedTransitions_PendingDowngradeAtPeriodEnd_AppliesDowngrade, full lifecycle scenarios). Tests/PlanTests.cs: 10 tests.

assertions.text: IDateTimeProvider or equivalent time abstraction used for testability
assertions.notes: IDateTimeProvider interface defined in domain layer, injected into SubscriptionService constructor, substituted with NSubstitute in SubscriptionServiceTests to control time precisely.
assertions.evidence: Domain/IDateTimeProvider.cs: `public interface IDateTimeProvider { DateTime UtcNow { get; } }`. Services/SubscriptionService.cs line 12: `private readonly IDateTimeProvider _dateTime;`. Tests/SubscriptionServiceTests.cs line 16-17: `_dateTime = Substitute.For<IDateTimeProvider>(); _dateTime.UtcNow.Returns(Now);`.
